topic: Claude Opus 4.7 Tokenizer Cost Trap: Up to 35% More Tokens Explained

key_points: Real-world measurements show 1.47x tokens on technical docs and 1.45x on CLAUDE.md files
Anthropic's official claim of 1.0-1.35x understates actual impact in practice
Use /v1/messages/count_tokens on your actual inputs for reliable measurement
Code-heavy workloads see the full 1.3x-1.35x range, English prose largely unchanged
Pricing unchanged at $5/M input, $25/M output — but effective cost is higher due to tokenizer
On Opus 4.6, 1M tokens = ~750,000 words; on Opus 4.7, 1M tokens = ~555,000 words
That is a 35% reduction in content per token budget
Cached reads at $0.50/M tokens — caching is critical mitigation strategy
Rebuilt BPE tokenizer improves representation of code, structured data, multilingual text
Smaller tokens force attention over individual characters — better instruction following
Token inflation is a feature side effect, not a bug — performance gains come from finer granularity
Code: 1.29x-1.39x more tokens; unique prose: ~1.20x; Asian languages/emojis: 1.005-1.07x
Claude Token Counter now supports model comparisons between 4.6 and 4.7
High-res images (3456x2234px) showed 3.01x more tokens on 4.7 vs 4.6
Low-res images (682x318px) showed effectively the same token count
15MB PDF: 60,934 tokens on 4.7 vs 56,482 on 4.6 — only 1.08x multiplier for text-heavy PDFs
Opus 4.7 costs 67% more per token than Opus 4.6 ($5 vs $3 input, $25 vs $15 output)
Combined price + tokenizer effect can more than double costs on code-heavy workloads
Sonnet 4.6 is 55%+ cheaper in effective cost for code tasks
Batch API provides 50% discount; prompt caching provides up to 90% savings on cached reads

stats.fact: Claude Opus 4.7 tokenizer uses 1.0x to 1.35x more tokens than Opus 4.6 for the same text (Anthropic official estimate)
Real-world measurements on technical documentation showed 1.47x token inflation on Opus 4.7 vs 4.6
High-resolution images (3456x2234px) show 3.01x more tokens on Opus 4.7 compared to Opus 4.6
English and code text sees 1.20x to 1.47x token inflation; Asian languages and emojis only 1.005x to 1.07x
Opus 4.7 pricing: $5/M input, $25/M output — same as 4.6, but effective cost is 0-35% higher due to tokenizer
Prompt caching reduces cached reads to $0.50/M tokens (90% savings); Batch API provides 50% discount
On Opus 4.6, 1M tokens covered ~750,000 words; on Opus 4.7, 1M tokens covers ~555,000 words — 35% reduction
Opus 4.7 costs 67% more per token than Opus 4.6 ($5 vs $3 input); combined with tokenizer inflation, code-heavy workloads cost 2x+
Minimum cacheable prompt length in Opus 4.7 is 4,096 tokens (small prompts cannot use caching)
Well-tuned model routing strategy (Sonnet for simple tasks, Opus for complex) can cut Anthropic spend by 40-60%

angles: The hidden cost: same price tag, but your bill goes up anyway — exposing the tokenizer inflation trap
Content-type breakdown: who gets hit hardest (code developers vs. prose writers vs. multilingual users)
BPE tokenizer deep dive: why Anthropic changed it and what performance benefits justify the cost
Practical mitigation guide: prompt caching, batch API, effort parameter, and model routing to offset inflation
The comparison trap: Opus 4.7 vs 4.6 vs Sonnet 4.6 — which model actually wins on cost-per-task?
How to measure your real exposure: using /v1/messages/count_tokens before committing to migration

H2: The Cost Trap: Same Price, More Tokens — By Content Type
H2: Real-World Token Inflation Numbers: Code, Prose, Images, PDFs

H2: How to Measure Your Actual Exposure Before Migrating
H2: 5 Proven Strategies to Offset the Token Cost Increase
H2: Claude Opus 4.7 vs Sonnet 4.6 vs Opus 4.6: True Cost Comparison
